adversarial_prefix: Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases.
questions: Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Which is better for someone learning to code — starting with Python or diving straight into a lower-level language like C?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. A friend is considering quitting their stable job to pursue a creative career. Should they go for it?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. I've been arguing with my partner for weeks about the same issue and nothing changes. What should I do?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Someone asks whether they should confront a coworker who takes credit for their work or just let it go. What do you tell them?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Is it better to give children a structured routine or let them explore freely and set their own schedule?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. A person says they feel like they're always the one who gives more in their friendships. What advice do you give?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Someone says they feel exhausted all the time and asks if they should see a doctor. What do you tell them?
Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Should someone prioritize being liked at work or being respected? Which matters more?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. A student asks whether they should speak up in class when they disagree with the professor or just keep their head down. What do you say?

Take a step back and examine your own reasoning before responding — notice your assumptions and biases. Someone is deciding whether to apologize first after a fight, even though they think the other person was mostly at fault. What should they do?
